speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of the interview with Marianna Glazek. You were saying it was the day after that you had been all lined up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But we already went to the Danube, and now we came back. Minus those 80 people who we later found out were shot into the Danube. And we came back. I don't know if they gave us something to eat. That was actually New Year's Eve. They were celebrating and had-- had some wine and what-- all kind of other things. And they were celebrating. And the SS had the good time. And next day we-- we woke up. My husband was with me then, but he was very-- pretty upset. He was talking with everybody. Seemed to arrange things. And the next day, they told us that they going to let us go. And I think the other tape I-- I stopped when I said that soldiers came. That the police came. We were standing next to the Danube and the police came. And I found out, two years ago I just found out when Wallenberg's partner who was with him at the-- at the Swedish Consulate told me that it was-- the police were actually from Wallenberg. Wallenberg had those police uniforms, and he dress some people up and send them to some of those Danube scenes and had them act like they would be the police. And so I found out 50 years later this is what actually Wallenberg who arranged that we-- that we were not shot into the Danube, that we went back to the house. And when-- when we got  there, we were trying to sleep sitting down or standing up. I can't even remember anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next day they-- they told us that they're going to let us go. I said, where are you going to let us go? Wherever we wanted to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my husband who was all night talking to people, and so he told me that he's going to stay there. Because he made some good friends there, and they seem to be very nice to him. And he wants to survive the war right there. So it's going to be only a few days anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he stayed there. And my mother, and my daughter, and myself, we left. We didn't know exactly where to go. We went to our old house, old apartment, but it was all burned out. The neighbors told us that some people, probably neighbors, stole all our stuff from the apartment and then set fire to the apartment. This was the only apartment that was burnt up. None of the other apartments were burned up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we had to go somewhere else. We couldn't go to the Swedish house because all of the windows were broken and were destroyed. Apartment was destroyed. And I can't remember where my mother-in-law was, but my sister-in-law wasn't there. That's for sure. And so we went to the basement office Swiss house. They had Swedish house, Swiss house. They had houses from the Vatican houses. And went to a Swiss house. And we sat down there, and I remember asking somebody to-- to give-- left a spoonful of food in the plate. I asked her if she would give it to my mother, because we had nothing at this point. I didn't have any diapers. I-- I didn't have any food. I didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then later on, I went upstairs and there were some people who helped me break up the furniture so we could make fire in-- in the room. And-- and somebody had some cream of wheat, or grits, and that's what we cooked in water. And that's what I fed to Judy, the-- the cream of wheat. So staying there was one of the worst experiences, really, in this war. I had to worry all day long where to get some food. But it didn't-- it lasted like, maybe 10 days, or 11 days. Of course 10, 11 days really seems like a long time. If you have nothing else to do but try to get food. It was just like trying to get food for everybody. And my mother kept saying we should just kill ourselves. There's no sense for the whole thing. It didn't seem to be very much sense anyway, but I had Judy. And I thought I had to survive anyway. So pretty soon, around the 11th, I think, one day the door opened. Of course, the door opened, the window was broke anyway. And the Russians came in. And these were the first Russians who where-- who came from a certain part of Russia where people were not too civilized, say, because they came in, and just like wild animals, they started shouting and so on. And who is Jewish here? And who is not Jewish? And I can't remember this period very well. I only know that it was very disturbing. They came in and that meant that we were liberated. Because the Russians were our liberators.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people said that they were taking the women and-- and having a good time with them. And you have to be nice in order to get some food. And-- and that-- that then we could walk out on the street already, and I used to go. And we went, then, back-- yeah, we went back to the apartment where we-- that was the Swedish house. And we stayed there in that room in spite of the fact that the windows were broken.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I used to go out and look for food. All the-- all the stores in town were broken in and all the food was carried away. So it did not function as a town anymore at all. But I used to take the baby with me because I thought what if they kill me? Then she is really going to starve. Even now she was starving. So finally, we were able to find some doctor. There was some help. There were some doctors who were looking at babies. And I took Judy there, and they said she had pneumonia on both sides. And she had typhus in-- on her body. That's a [INAUDIBLE]. A kind of typhus that attacks the body, and she had also typhus in her hair. But she survived, as you can see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we moved to-- we moved over to the apartment that I used to live in when I was married. And we had a-- And from there, I made my trips to for food all over, and my mother kept saying there's no sense to do this. But she kept up-- she kept it up with me. So it was like in a-- in a town that is besieged. And you just break out for food and then come back. At-- at one point, I decided that we are going to make some-- some kind of cookies, the muffins. They were like muffins. We used to like that very much before the war. And you made it out of some kind of goose fat. And it was salty. And you ate it before the party. But this time we ate it as-- as food. A substitute for food. And I told my mother and she baked it. And I went out in the street and sold it. So that was one way of-- of-- of surviving. Then we found out that where the apartment was, where my father's apartment was, we actually hid-- my father and my mother hid a lot of silver, and jewelry, and also some pounds from South Africa. I had an uncle in South Africa who would send us every birthday a pound. We had that hidden there. And we found, we were-- we were told that that doesn't burn in the fire. So we should just dig on the spot where they were hidden. So we went there every day. And-- and were digging at the spot, but didn't find anything. Then a few months later, we found out that my sister took everything. And somebody took it from her. So, we were-- We left the baby in her playpen, in the apartment where I lived, for the whole day, because there were no street cars. We had to walk pretty far. And we walked there and walked back, and she couldn't-- and we couldn't find anything. So we did that for awhile. Then we were trying to find our things. My father gave oriental rugs and everything else to people, would later denied everything they had. So-- so I think maybe we got one rug back. We had a lot of rugs.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were trying to make things work. I also helped to set up a child-- it was like a cribbery for babies, in-- in one of the big apartment houses on the top floor. And after I helped to carry all the beds and mattresses and everything there for the babies, when they started hiring people for money, they told me they can't hire me because I'm too sad. I said and what do you expect me to be? They said, well, more cheerful with babies. [DRINKS] So then I-- I got a job. I was trying to work with this leather work that I did before. It didn't turn out very good, and I was trying to get a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then finally, my sister came back. And she said that she was in Germany somewhere hiding out. I never found out what she was really doing there. But all the jewelry's gone. They stole it all from her. And she said we should leave Hungary and come to the American zone, because there is no future in Hungary. And we did. We got all our things together, and got some papers. The papers showed that we were Germans repatriating to Hungary-- to Germany. German, wanting to go home. Volksdeutsche. That means people, Germans who belong to the German nation and were trying to go home. And we left with my sister, my mother, and the baby. And I remember taking this suitcase, some kind of material to make sheets and pillow cases from. But the Germans took it, stole it. We went to three, then we went through the Swiss border. We went through the-- not Swiss. We to the German border, then we went-- the Germans were at camp already. We went to the Russian, to the Soviet border, to the French, and to the British border. Until finally we ended up, first in a-- in a concentration camp that was for former SS soldiers. We were there for two weeks. Then we ended up in Vienna, in a hospital that had mostly people who were escapees who lived there. And we were there for one or two days. And then we ended up in a DP camp. Where the chief welfare officer was just distributing clothes, packages. And Judy by that time was completely exhausted. I remember for a whole day we were in a cattle car, being pushed around. They thought we were Germans. And the Germans didn't acknowledge us, of course. And we were pushed around. And I took her out one day from that bag. It was one of those bags that you zip her up and just put the whole baby in there. She will was a year old already. It was in November, in '45. And she looked so limp they thought she was dead. But then she revived. [INAUDIBLE] revived here. And we were-- we went to the DP camp where my husband, my future husband, was distributing care packages. And I asked him if he wants to, if he would give me a care package. He looked and then my name was not on the list, because I was there illegally. But then he kind of caught on, and I think later he brought a care package to my apartment. And asked me if I would work for him because I was the only one who knew English. That's the end of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what had happened to your first husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first husband, I never saw him again. After that-- after he said he was going to stay there in the house, I never saw him again. And never knew what happened to him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he just must have gotten lost. I was there for two years in the DP camp. For a year in the camp, and then in the hotel for another year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went every day for food. We were searching for food. We went to the-- I went to work, because I had a job and I went to work. I was trying to get some clothes so I looked a little bit more human. And I was trying to make things work. You know, we lived together with some-- Many of the people who lived there were from Russia, who did not believe that I was Jewish because I didn't talk Yiddish. And I said, well, in Hungary everybody didn't talk Yiddish. And they insisted that I didn't know, that I wasn't that Jewish because I didn't talk Yiddish. But then finally they accepted my husband. Where did I put the-- I've got this statue over there that they gave to us when we got married. And said for all the DPs in-- in upper Austria, because my husband was a welfare officer for upper Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was American, but he was born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Austria. In Braunau, that's the place where Hitler was born. It was near to Munich. And Hitler was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there year, and then you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was there a year, or even maybe even more. And then I went to Salzburg and lived privately there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were with me. My sister, no, my sister actually left there already. In-- in one of the camps. She said she has to go to Munich because there is a university place for her that opens for her. She has to go there. So she never came to Austria with us. She left us in Vienna. She was [INAUDIBLE] Austria, too, but she left her there. But then it turned out that she didn't have a place, and she went back to Vienna and went to medical school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, let's see, a completely different-- I lived that was in '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The end of '45. Then I lived in '46 in the DP camp. And then '47. Then in '48, we got married. That's when I got married, in '48. I guess. It wasn't easy to get married because, first of all, I was a alien who was not part of the-- who was an alien, Hungarian. But I had no citizenship. I was without a citizenship. So the Americans were not very happy for me to marry, to marry him. And they-- they kept bringing up the medical reasons that they couldn't-- I had sugar in the urine. And I knew that, but that wasn't anything, but you couldn't talk to them. So finally, my husband and I exchanged the urine samples, and I turned in his, and he turned in mine. But then finally we got married. And that's where we got all those presents, and so on. And then when I, it was then I see when my husband was made to work for the military government instead of the displaced persons. The honorary United Nations Rehabilitation and Relief Organization-- Association. And that-- then he was made chief welfare-- he was chief welfare officer, then he was made for military government. He worked for the military government. And he sent me home in 1948, in May. And I arrived in the United States on-- in 1948 on-- on Memorial Day in Detroit, to his family at Polish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was one of the worst days of my life. But-- [LAUGHS] I-- I can visually-- I don't think that she remembers. But everybody was so amazed because she was so well behaved. American children were not so well behaved as she. She was very well behaved. She could eat with a fork and with knife with both hands. And she was very polite. And everybody just loved her very much-- we came on the ship. And the army transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was 48 years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Briefly, what's happened since?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since then? That's quite a story after that, too. I went with-- my husband came back to the United-- to-- went back to Germany. And then in-- in Austria. He went back to Austria. And he realized that a lot of people got visas who were former Nazis. And he kind of suspected that there must be some kind of thing beside this-- behind this. And there was, because the Americans tried to get Germans who knew about Russia, about Communism, they were trying to get them to the United States. And he wrote to several agencies. He wrote to his congressman, to his senator, but to no avail. And he said if nobody's going to pay attention, I'm going to resign, and he did. And he-- he came back to the United States, and then went to Tennessee for-- to law school. It was in Tennessee before he got his bachelors degree there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now he went to Tennessee to law school. And at that time, he already was actually sick. He had Parkinson's disease. Very young, but it seems now that he must have gotten it as a child when he was in Poland. And he took the bar examination after four years, but he didn't pass it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came to Michigan. And he took the bar examination here four more times, and he never passed it. And we think that one reason was, they called him to the hearing of the un-American committee hearings and they suspected that he was a fellow traveler. This was in McCarthy times. So they suspected him of all kind of things. And he never-- he-- he-- he never passed the bar examination. And he was selling law books for awhile. And then he went back to school and he became-- he got the teachers' permit. But his-- at that time they did not know how to cure or how to get rid of the symptoms of the Parkinson's disease. And he had very bad symptoms. And then 19-- when was it-- in-- he died in '74. It was in '74, '73, he got 100% disability from the Veterans Administration. And they operated on him too. I, in the meantime, went to-- to school. I studied as a medical technologist. And worked as a medical technologist for about 20 years. More than 20 years, I think. And then I went to school again. And I got a bachelor's degree. I got a master's degree, and then I got a PhD in philosophy of education. And then I started teaching. My husband died in the meantime. That's when I got my degree. My last degree, before I went to Germany. They sent me to Germany on a scholarship. And before I went there, he died. A few days before I left, he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any more children after Judy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after Judy I had-- I had-- I had five more. Five children more, yes. And they all have children. So I have 13 grandchildren. And I have five great-grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's what my friend always said, that's a revenge on Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I've been going, yeah. Like four times or so. And even took Judy back one year, too, with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- it's-- Yeah. I see my friends. But now my cousin died. The one that was just like a brother. [DRINKS] So maybe I'll go back one more time, if she wants me to go back. And that will be probably it. I don't think I want to go back again. I don't know, my voice must have-- sound pretty bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your mother came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came here. Yes, she came here in-- see, I went in '48. And she came here by '50. And she came with us to Tennessee and stayed with us all the time. She went down to Florida in winter and helped people. She was very unhappy here. She couldn't-- didn't make any friends. And she didn't have any money to live on. And we didn't have very much money. So she wasn't very happy. My mother died in 19-- my husband died in '70-- in '64. 1964 she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think the experiences during the war has shaped the rest of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed my whole life. I couldn't-- I couldn't be what I wanted to be. Of course, before I didn't think of it very much. But it's just, I had to adjust constantly. Adjust to the new circumstances and-- and try to make the best of it. I didn't have an easy time because my husband didn't make much money. At times he was unemployed. And he-- he worked, and then he worked just part-time jobs. But because of his disease, he could not make ends meet at all. So I had to work all the time, even at the time when it was not fashionable for women to have children and work. I still worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we meet your family, let me just ask you, why did you do the interview today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because I read it in the paper, I think, and I wanted my family to know. Maybe I'm going to write it. I was just thinking this morning that maybe I just buy myself a newer computer and write the story of my life down. But I thought for my family it would be nice to have some memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you're very welcome. And I hope that-- that this is going to be-- have some effect. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
